messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"cra-conformity-clock","title":"Cyber Resilience Act's Sept-2026 reporting and Dec-2027 conformity deadlines turn Year-1 large-operator tenders into CRA-readiness screens","narrative":"An intelligent, networked PDU is a product-with-digital-elements under Regulation (EU) 2024/2847. Vulnerability-reporting obligations land 11 September 2026 — inside the venture's first 12 months — and full conformity (CRA CE marking, technical file, SBOM, coordinated vulnerability disclosure) lands 11 December 2027. Sophisticated buyers do not wait for legal deadlines: large operators are already writing CRA clauses into 2026 procurement frameworks. With CRA harmonized standards not yet published and notified-body capacity scarce, a pre-launch entrant cannot close a CRA-credible technical file inside the entry window. The serviceableFactor evidence names only EN 50600, ecodesign 2019/424 and CE/TÜV — the newest and most binding instrument for THIS product class is absent from the model entirely.","category":"exogenous","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor","obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"CRA (Reg. 2024/2847) applies to networked PDUs as products-with-digital-elements: vulnerability-reporting duties from 11 Sep 2026, full conformity assessment (SBOM, secure-by-design technical file, CVD process) from 11 Dec 2027 — dates that overlap or immediately shadow the pre-launch entrant's first 12 months","refs":["profile:Product","profile:Stage","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c10"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Large operators — the venture's declared first channel — front-load CRA-readiness (SBOM delivery, vulnerability-handling attestations) into 2026 RFPs; absent published harmonized standards, demonstrating conformity requires scarce notified-body engagement, so the entrant is screened out of frameworks the incumbents (with existing product-security organizations) pass","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c12","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The serviceableFactor haircut is justified by a three-instrument cert stack that omits the CRA; both reach (which demand is regulatory-clear for us) and Year-1 pace (how fast tenders can convert) are overstated for a networked product launching into the CRA transition window","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The CRA reads as 'just another CE marking' and its headline deadline (Dec 2027) sits outside the 12-month window on paper — so it gets filed under 'later'. The kill path is buyer procurement clocks running 12–18 months ahead of legal deadlines, plus the reporting obligation that actually lands mid-window.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A sample of 2026 CE large-operator rack-power RFPs/framework agreements containing no CRA, SBOM or secure-development clauses, combined with confirmation that intelligent PDUs fall in the CRA default class with pure self-assessment and the entrant's CRA technical file is completable pre-launch.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"The dates and product-scope are enacted law, not forecast; the only uncertainty is how fast CE operators pull CRA requirements into procurement, and regulated buyers historically front-run compliance deadlines with supplier asks.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.85,"note":"CRA-gated demand (security-clause frameworks) drops out of the reachable set until the conformity file closes"},{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.02,"note":"tender cycles extend while CRA evidence is assembled; Year-1 pace slows below the 3% mid-band"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"CRA/SBOM/secure-development clauses appearing in operator rack-power procurement","where":"TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) notices; supplier-requirement pages of Equinix/NTT/Vantage/Data4 procurement portals","threshold":"≥2 CE operator RFPs requiring SBOM delivery or CRA conformity declaration before Dec 2026","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Publication status of CRA harmonized standards (CEN/CENELEC JTC 13 work programme)","where":"OJEU harmonized-standards listings; CEN/CENELEC JTC 13 docket","threshold":"hENs for horizontal CRA requirements slipping past mid-2026, forcing notified-body routes","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a CRA classification and gap assessment (default vs important-product class, self-assessment vs notified-body route) for the PDU's connectivity stack","type":"information","voiNodeId":"serviceableFactor","note":"determines whether the conformity clock even fits the entry window"},{"action":"Stand up SBOM generation and an IEC 62443-4-1-aligned secure development lifecycle now, and pre-book a notified-body assessment slot before the 2026 queue forms","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Sequence first sales through an OEM/integrator whose CRA-compliant product wrapper covers the integrated PDU while the entrant's own file matures","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"eichrecht-metering-foreclosure","title":"German legal metrology (MessEG/MessEV) forecloses billing-grade outlet metering — the intelligent PDU's core value proposition — in the model's largest geography","narrative":"Half of this funnel is Germany, and the product's premium is 'intelligent' — per-outlet metering. But in Germany, any meter whose output becomes an invoice line (colo tenant power billing, chargeback) must be conformity-assessed under the Mess- und Eichgesetz — a legal-metrology regime entirely separate from CE/LVD/TÜV product safety. Incumbents ship Eichrecht/MID-certified metering options; a new entrant without type examination cannot lawfully anchor billing, demoting its metering to 'indicative only' and stripping the differentiation exactly where the model concentrates its weight. The certification path runs 12–18 months — past the YAM window. EED 2024/1791's KPI-disclosure regime is simultaneously institutionalizing measured energy data, making certified metering more, not less, decisive.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor","geo.DE"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"German colo operators billing tenants on measured kWh must use legally-verified meters under MessEG/MessEV; PDU-embedded outlet metering without PTB-recognized type examination cannot serve as the billing basis in the market carrying ~1,955 MW of installed IT power","refs":["profile:Product","digest:geo-DE#c13"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"geo.DE carries 0.50 of the funnel, and EED 2024/1791 KPI/energy-disclosure duties are pushing operators toward invoice-grade measured power; competitors with certified metering SKUs win the metered-billing use case, relegating the entrant to non-billing deployments in DE colocation","refs":["ledger:geo.DE","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c9","ledger:seg.colocation"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The reachable share of the German half of the funnel is overstated: billing-grade metered deployments are foreclosed until a ~12–18-month legal-metrology certification completes, i.e. after the Year-1 window closes — a reach haircut the 0.55 serviceableFactor never priced","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The team's compliance sweep covers product-safety and energy law (LVD, EMC, ecodesign, EN 50600, TÜV) — metering law lives in a different legal family (legal metrology) that only bites when the meter output becomes an invoice. It is invisible until a German procurement asks 'ist das eichrechtskonform?', which happens after the sales cycle has already consumed months.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"CE colocation procurement specs and billing-practice interviews showing tenant power billing is anchored at feed/branch-circuit facility meters, with PDU outlet metering treated as informational only — making Eichrecht status irrelevant to the PDU purchase decision.","likelihood":{"value":0.3,"rationale":"The legal requirement is certain; the uncertainty is how much DE colo billing actually rides on PDU-level metering versus upstream branch meters. Given rising per-rack/per-outlet chargeback in high-density AI colocation, a material minority of the premium segment is plausibly gated.","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.82,"note":"billing-grade metered deployments in DE (and DE-influenced procurement in AT/CH) drop out of the Year-1 reachable set"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"'Eichrechtskonform'/MID-certified metering requirements appearing in DE colo fit-out RFQs","where":"TED notices; Frankfurt/Berlin colocation fit-out RFQs and operator technical specifications","threshold":"metering-certification clauses in ≥2 DE colo tenders","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Incumbent PDU vendors marketing legally-certified metering options","where":"Bachmann, Rittal, Legrand/Raritan German price lists and datasheets","threshold":"≥2 major vendors adding MID/Eichrecht-certified metering SKUs","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a legal-metrology opinion plus 5 structured interviews on DE colo billing practice (where in the power chain is the invoice anchored?)","type":"information","voiNodeId":"serviceableFactor","note":"cheap, fast, and decisive for whether the foreclosure is real"},{"action":"Reposition outlet metering as capacity-management/DCIM telemetry (not billing) and partner with a certified branch-meter vendor for billing use cases","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Start the PTB/notified-body type-examination path in parallel with launch so the billing-grade SKU lands in Year 2","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"nis2-avl-gate-large-operators","title":"NIS2 supply-chain clauses turn large operators' approved-vendor lists into a security-certification gate the pre-launch entrant cannot pass in Year 1","narrative":"The venture's declared first channel is large operators — the cust.operator-large node at 0.40, itself an unsourced internal estimate. But large CE data-center operators are essential entities under NIS2 (transposing across CE through 2024–26, Germany via the NIS2 implementation act), and Article 21(2)(d) obliges them to manage ICT supply-chain risk. Operationally this arrives as supplier-security questionnaires demanding ISO/IEC 27001 and IEC 62443 evidence for any networked device that touches their management plane — which an intelligent PDU does by definition. A pre-launch entrant holds none of these artifacts. The law never names PDU vendors, so it appears on no vendor risk list; it closes the primary channel anyway, forcing deals through OEM/integrator wrappers at lower margin and slower pace.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"NIS2 transposition makes large DC operators essential entities with legally-mandated supply-chain security duties; procurement operationalizes this as mandatory supplier security certifications (ISO 27001, IEC 62443-4-1/-4-2) for networked equipment on the management network — the category an intelligent PDU sits in","refs":["profile:Product","ledger:cust.operator-large","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c12"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The 0.40 buyer-mix estimate rests on capacity shares and qualitative channel logic with no evidence of how much large-operator spend sits behind certified approved-vendor lists; a pre-launch entrant with no certification history fails AVL onboarding regardless of product quality, and the sales motion migrates to OEM/integrators whose certifications wrap the product","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","ledger:cust.oem","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The large-operator slice reachable by this entrant in Year 1 is materially smaller than the modeled 0.40 — the funnel's largest buyer node overstates the addressable channel for precisely the venture's chosen entry path","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","profile:Stage"]}],"whyMissable":"NIS2 regulates operators, not vendors — a vendor-side regulatory checklist never surfaces it. It arrives disguised as a procurement questionnaire, not a law, and by the time the first AVL rejection lands, two quarters of the 12-month window are gone.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Supplier-onboarding documentation from ≥2 large CE operators showing networked rack-power vendors are admitted on self-declaration, without ISO 27001/IEC 62443 certification prerequisites.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"Large regulated buyers historically translate their own compliance duties into supplier certification requirements (finance, telecom, energy all followed this pattern); NIS2's supply-chain article makes DC operators the next instance. Uncertainty is the share of spend behind hard gates versus soft questionnaires.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"set","value":0.28,"note":"the certified-AVL-gated portion of large-operator PDU spend is unreachable for an uncertified entrant in the entry window — absolute shrink of this slice, not a mix shift"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Security-certification prerequisites in operator supplier-registration portals","where":"Equinix/NTT/Vantage/Data4 supplier onboarding portals; TED procurement notices for DC fit-outs","threshold":"ISO 27001 or IEC 62443 listed as mandatory for connected facility equipment","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"National NIS2 transposition guidance explicitly covering facility/OT equipment supply chains","where":"BSI (Germany) NIS2 guidance dockets; NCSC-NL publications","threshold":"guidance naming power/facility equipment vendors within supply-chain risk scope","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run the 3 structured CE operator/distributor procurement interviews already named as this node's promotion path, specifically probing AVL security prerequisites","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"replaces the unsourced 0.40 with a gate-adjusted, sourced buyer mix"},{"action":"Enter Year 1 via OEM/integrator channel whose certifications and AVL status wrap the product; treat direct large-operator sales as a Year-2 motion","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Begin a scope-limited ISO 27001 certification (product development and vulnerability handling) immediately — 9–12 months lead time means starting now or missing the window","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"per-country-access-stack","title":"The unmodeled per-country access stack — stiftung EAR WEEE numbers, packaging EPR, EORI, Swiss non-EU paperwork — serializes CE entry against a 12-month clock","narrative":"The serviceableFactor derivation admits its cert stack 'justifies <1.0 but doesn't quantify 0.55' and names only three regimes. The corpus itself surfaces a larger stack: WEEE registration (per-country, per-brand — Germany's stiftung EAR alone runs 2–4 months), packaging EPR (VerpackG/LUCID), EORI customs setup, RoHS/REACH documentation — and Switzerland sits outside the EU single market entirely, requiring separate importer and recycling (SENS) arrangements. None of these is individually a 'market risk'; the mechanism is serialization: each country becomes lawfully sellable only after its registrations complete, so within a 12-month window several geographies are unreachable for part or all of the year. That pushes effective Year-1 reach below the band's own low edge, and plausibly removes Switzerland (0.08 of the funnel) from the first-year set altogether.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor","geo.CH"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"First sale in each CE country requires completed country-level registrations: DE WEEE via stiftung EAR (ElektroG), packaging EPR (LUCID), EORI, plus non-EU Switzerland's separate importer/recycling regime — a stack the corpus documents as materially broader than the three regimes the model cites","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c10","ledger:geo.CH"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The digest's own tension flags that the fuller stack pushes serviceableFactor toward or below 0.45; but the deeper effect is temporal — registrations serialize country launches, so a pre-launch entrant's lawfully-reachable geography expands stepwise across the very 12 months the YAM measures","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c12","profile:Stage"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Year-1 serviceable reach sits below the 0.45 band edge, and the Swiss slice — with its distinct non-EU paperwork and self-operation-heavy buyer base — falls out of the first-year reachable scope entirely","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:geo.CH"]}],"whyMissable":"Each registration is individually trivial — a form and a few thousand euros — so none makes a risk list organized by 'big' instruments. The kill mechanism is their serialization against a fixed 12-month window, which only becomes visible when someone builds the compliance Gantt chart against the sales plan.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A counsel-built compliance timeline showing all CE-7 registrations (EAR, LUCID, national WEEE equivalents, EORI, Swiss importer/SENS setup) are completable in parallel within 90 days pre-launch.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"The registrations are legally mandatory and their lead times are documented administrative facts; the base rate of hardware entrants underestimating multi-country EPR/WEEE sequencing is high. Uncertainty is only whether the venture has quietly already started them.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"set","value":0.42,"note":"serialized country access holds Year-1 reach below the band's low edge"},{"nodeId":"geo.CH","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"Swiss non-EU compliance setup defers CH to Year 2 — foreclosed from this venture's Year-1 scope, not shrunk"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"stiftung EAR registration processing times and backlog","where":"stiftung EAR portal and published registration statistics","threshold":"processing exceeding 3 months for new B2B registrants","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Whether incumbents maintain distinct Swiss-compliance SKUs and importer structures","where":"Bachmann/Rittal/Vertiv Swiss price lists and Swiss distributor catalogues","threshold":"separate CH SKUs/importer-of-record arrangements visible at ≥2 incumbents","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a country-by-country market-access memo (registrations, lead times, dependencies) for the CE-7 and convert it into a bottom-up reachable-share model — the node's own promotion path","type":"information","voiNodeId":"serviceableFactor","note":"replaces the unanchored 0.55 with a dated, per-country reach schedule"},{"action":"File DE and NL registrations immediately (covering 0.78 of the funnel) and deliberately sequence CH/PL to Year 2","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Contract an EU authorized-representative/importer-of-record service to parallelize the remaining country stacks","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"enefg-grid-slip-de-demand","title":"EnEfG efficiency mandates plus 24–36-month Frankfurt grid queues push German new-build PDU orders past the YAM window while the snapshot TAM stays flat","narrative":"PDU revenue recognizes at fit-out and energization — not at announcement. Germany's Energy Efficiency Act (EnEfG) imposes PUE ≤1.2 and waste-heat-reuse duties on data centres commencing operation from mid-2026, forcing design rework on exactly the pipeline the entrant would sell into; simultaneously Frankfurt grid connections face documented 24–36-month waits and FLAP-D prices are rising. The market data that looks like strength — record-low 6.3% vacancy, 83% pre-let pipeline — actually means replacement demand cannot compensate for slipped new-build fit-outs inside 12 months. geo.DE's 0.50 is a 2025 capacity snapshot; the Year-1 orderable German slice is smaller and slower than the funnel implies, and Germany is half the funnel.","category":"exogenous","targetNodes":["geo.DE","obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"EnEfG PUE/waste-heat mandates bite for German DCs commencing operation from mid-2026 (with EED 2024/1791 layering KPI disclosure), while Frankfurt — ~60–75% of German capacity — faces 24–36-month grid-connection waits and 12% FLAP-D price rises in 2026","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c9","digest:geo-DE#c20","digest:geo-DE#c1"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"New-build fit-outs — the moment rack PDUs are specified and ordered — slip as designs are reworked for compliance and energization dates move right; with vacancy at a record-low 6.3% and the pipeline 83% pre-let, there is no slack inventory of halls generating near-term replacement PDU demand to offset the slip","refs":["digest:geo-DE#c21","ledger:geo.DE"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The German half of the funnel is a static 2025 snapshot: the Year-1 orderable DE slice is smaller in absolute terms and converts slower than geo.DE 0.50 × obtainableFactor 0.03 implies — a timing hollow-out invisible in the level-based bands","refs":["ledger:geo.DE","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"Every headline the founder reads — record-low vacancy, pre-let pipelines, AI demand — files under upside. The list logs 'booming German market' as a strength and never inverts it: PDU revenue lands at fit-out/energization, which is precisely the step that regulation and grid queues delay.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"2026 quarterly Frankfurt/Berlin market reports (JLL/CBRE) showing white-space fit-outs and energizations proceeding on original schedules despite EnEfG requirements and grid constraints, with under-construction MW converting to live MW at historical rates.","likelihood":{"value":0.35,"rationale":"Grid waits of 24–36 months and EnEfG dates are documented; JLL data already shows a large gap between live (745 MW) and under-construction/planned Frankfurt capacity. The uncertainty is the magnitude of Year-1 order slippage, not its existence.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"geo.DE","op":"set","value":0.45,"note":"Year-1 orderable DE slice smaller in absolute terms as new-build fit-outs slip past the window — deliberate non-renormalization: absolute shrink, not mix shift"},{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.8,"note":"conversion pace slows as pipeline orders defer to energization dates"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Announced energization/commissioning slips at flagship German builds","where":"JLL German data-center quarterly reports; TenneT/Amprion grid-connection announcements; operator capex calls (e.g., Vantage, NTT GDC Europe)","threshold":"≥2 flagship Frankfurt/Berlin builds announcing 6+ month slips to energization","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"EnEfG enforcement and transition-waiver practice for data centres","where":"BfEE/BAFA guidance dockets; German Datacenter Association (germandatacenters.com) position papers","threshold":"strict application of PUE/heat-reuse rules to 2026 commencements without transitional waivers","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Survey order-timing across the 2026 German fit-out pipeline (when are PDUs specified vs energized) to date the DE revenue window directly","type":"information","voiNodeId":"geo.DE","note":"converts the snapshot 0.50 into a dated orderable share"},{"action":"Weight the Year-1 pipeline toward NL and PL, where efficiency mandates are lighter and capacity growth is faster","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Build a retrofit/refresh offer for operating halls (monitoring upgrades in live capacity) that is insensitive to new-build energization slips","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]}]}
